title: Studio OS
tagline: A personal life operating system designed as a warm 'digital room'
coverImageAlt: Studio OS home — a dark, warm 'digital room' dashboard
problem: Most productivity tools feel like an admin dashboard — cold, demanding, and easy to abandon. I wanted a personal system that felt like coming home: somewhere to see the day's focus, protect what matters first, and keep small promises without guilt.
context: Studio OS is a personal life-operating-system I designed and built for myself, then redesigned around a 'digital room' metaphor — zones for today's focus, study, building, a calm corner, and a long-term horizon. It is fully offline: all state lives in localStorage, ambient focus music is synthesised live in the browser, and there is no backend or account. The public demo runs on a neutral demo persona so the concept can be explored without any of my private data.
constraints: Had to work fully offline with no backend, account, or API keys
Should feel warm and personal, not like a SaaS admin panel
Must stay genuinely useful day-to-day, not just look nice
The public demo must contain none of my real personal data
myRole: I designed the concept, the visual language, and built the whole thing — the room layout, the time-aware sessions, the local-first state engine, and a build-flagged demo-content layer that keeps my real data private.
solution: A single-page 'digital room' built on local-first state with defensive localStorage normalisers and SSR guards. The UI is time-aware (it suggests the right zone for the current hour), respects reduced-motion, and ships a Web-Audio ambient-music engine with no audio files. A NEXT_PUBLIC_DEMO build flag swaps in a neutral demo persona so the deployed version exposes the design without exposing me.
callout: The public demo is privacy-safe by construction: a build flag tree-shakes my real content out entirely and swaps in a neutral demo persona, while my private version stays local-only.
tradeoffs: Chose local-first (localStorage) over a backend — instant, private, and offline, at the cost of cross-device sync.
Synthesised ambient music with the Web Audio API instead of shipping audio files, keeping the bundle tiny and license-free.
Built a demo-content layer gated by a build flag rather than maintaining two codebases.
whatIBuilt: A 'digital room' dashboard with distinct zones for focus, study, building, reset, and a long-term horizon.
A time-aware home that suggests the right activity for the current part of the day.
A local-first state layer with defensive JSON normalisers and safe parsing so bad data never crashes the app.
A Web-Audio ambient-music engine with focus modes, generated entirely in the browser.
A build-flagged demo-content layer plus a 'reset to demo data' control for visitors.
whatILearned: How to build a genuinely offline, local-first app with resilient state handling.
Using a build flag and tree-shaking to ship a privacy-safe public build from a private codebase.
Generating ambient audio with the Web Audio API instead of bundling media.
Designing for warmth and emotion in a tool — accessibility, reduced-motion, and tone all matter.
That the hardest part of a personal tool is making it calm enough that you actually keep using it.
results: Fully offline and credential-free; lint, strict type-check, and production build all pass clean.
The deployed demo build contains zero private personal data (verified by build-output scan).
Every route prerenders as fast static content with a small client bundle.

screenshots.caption: The home 'room' — warm dark theme, zones, and the day's focus.
The daily focus view on a phone — small, honest proof for the day.
A calm reset corner — a short sequence to refocus when the day gets messy.
The long-term horizon — the 'why' behind the daily work.

repoNote: Validated with ESLint, strict TypeScript, and two clean builds (normal + demo); the demo build output was scanned to confirm zero private data ships.
